content.problemStatement:
**This is the easy version of the problem. The difference with the hard version is highlighted in bold.**

You are given a tree containing NNN vertices and N−1N-1N−1 undirected edges. You have to color each vertex of the tree either red, blue, or green.

Each vertex must receive exactly one color, and there should also be at least one vertex having each color in the end.

For a vertex uuu, define score(u)\text{score}(u)score(u) to be the sum of distances from uuu, to the closest vertices of the other two colors.

More clearly, if uuu is colored red, let d1d_1d1​ be the minimum distance from uuu to a green vertex, and d2d_2d2​ be the minimum distance from uuu to a blue vertex.
Then, we have

score(u)=d1+d2\text{score}(u) = d_1 + d_2score(u)=d1​+d2​The distance between two vertices uuu and vvv is equal to the number of edges on the simple path between uuu and vvv in the tree.

The beauty of the tree is calculated as the sum of score(u)\text{score}(u)score(u) across all vertices uuu.

**Your task is to output the minimum possible beauty of the tree that can be achieved over all valid colorings.**


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of vertices in the tree.
The next N−1N-1N−1 lines describe the edges. The iii-th of these N−1N-1N−1 lines contains two space-separated integers uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​, denoting an undirected edge between uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​.




Output Format
For each test case you have to output a single integer denoting the **minimum possible beauty** of the tree that can be achieved over all valid colorings, each on a new line.
constraints: The input edges describe a tree.
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Color vertex 111 red, vertex 222 blue, and vertex 333 green. Then,

score(1)\text{score}(1)score(1) depends on the closest blue and green vertices to 111, since it's red.
These are 222 and 333 respectively, at distances 111 and 222. So, score(1)=1+2=3\text{score}(1) = 1+2 = 3score(1)=1+2=3.
score(2)\text{score}(2)score(2) depends on the closest red and green vertices to 111, since it's blue.
These are 111 and 333 respectively, at distances 111 and 111. So, score(2)=1+1=2\text{score}(2) = 1+1 = 2score(2)=1+1=2.
score(3)\text{score}(3)score(3) depends on the closest blue and red vertices to 111, since it's green.
These are 222 and 111 respectively, at distances 111 and 222. So, score(3)=1+2=3\text{score}(3) = 1+2 = 3score(3)=1+2=3.

The sum of scores is 3+2+3=83+2+3 = 83+2+3=8. It can be proved that this is optimal.
Test case 222: Suppose we color vertex 333 red, vertex 222 blue, and vertices 1,4,51, 4, 51,4,5 all green. Then,

The scores of the vertices can be seen to be [3,2,2,3,3][3, 2, 2, 3, 3][3,2,2,3,3].
The beauty is the sum of all of these, which is 131313.

It can be proved that no valid coloring attains a beauty less than 131313.
